analogy: Think of "Planning and Sustainable Development" through the chapter's own opening images: Unit V Unit V Unit V Unit V Unit V Chapter 9 GEOGRAPHICAL PERSPECTIVE ON SELECTED ISSUES AND PROBLEMS En En En En Envir vir vir vir vironmental P onmental P onmental P onmental P onmental Pollution ollution ollution ollution ollution Environmental pollution results from... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Planning and Sustainable Development is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that unit V Unit V Unit V Unit V Unit V Chapter 9 GEOGRAPHICAL PERSPECTIVE ON SELECTED ISSUES AND PROBLEMS En En En En Envir vir vir vir vironmental P onmental P onmental P onmental P onmental Pollution ollution ollution ollution ollution Environmental pollution results from ‘the release of substances and energy from waste products of human activities.

The text explains that they are classified on the basis of medium through which pollutants are transported and diffused.

The text explains that pollution can be classified into (i) air pollution, (ii) water pollution, (iii) land pollution and (iv) noise pollution.

The text explains that water Pollution Indiscriminate use of water by increasing population and industrial expansion has led degradation of the quality of water considerably.

The text explains that surface water available from rivers, canals, lakes, etc. is never pure.

The text explains that it contains small quantities of suspended particles, organic and inorganic substances.

The text explains that when concentration of these substances increases, the water becomes polluted, and hence becomes unfit for use.

The text explains that in such a situation, the self-purifying capacity of water is unable to purify the water.

The text explains that though water pollutants are also created from natural sources (erosion, landslides, decay and decomposition of plants and animals, etc.) pollutants from human activities are the real causes of concern.

The text explains that human beings pollute the water through industrial, agricultural and cultural activities.

The text explains that among these activities, industry is the most significant contributor.

The text explains that 96 India : People and Economy Pollution Types Pollution Involved Sources of Pollution Air Pollution Oxides of sulphur (SO2, SO3), Oxides of nitrogen, carbon monoxide, hydro-carbon, ammonia, lead, aldehydes asbestos and beryllium.

The text explains that combustion of coal, petrol and diesel, industrial processes, solid waste disposal, sewage disposal, etc.

The text explains that water Pollution Odour, dissolved and suspended solids, ammonia and urea, nitrate and nitrites, chloride, fluoride, carbonates, oil and grease, insecticide and pesticide residue, tannin, coliform MPM (bacterial count) sulphates and sulphides, heavy metals e.g. lead, aresenic, mercury, manganese, etc., radioactive substances.

The text explains that sewage disposal, urban run-off, toxic effluents from industries, run-off over cultivated lands and nuclear power plants.

The text explains that land Pollution Human and animal excreta viruses and bacteria, garbage and vectors therein, pesticides and fertiliser-residue alkalinity, fluorides, radio-active substances.

The text explains that dumping of carcasses in the river Table 9.2 : Sources of Pollution in the Ganga and the Yamuna Rivers Cities of Kanpur, Prayagraj, Varanasi, Patna and Kolkata release domestic waste into the river Yamuna (Delhi) and (Uttar Pradesh) (a) Delhi to confluence with Chambal (b) Mathura and Agra 1.

The text explains that geographical Perspective on Selected Issues and Problems 97 surface water sources are contaminated and unfit for human consumption.

The text explains that water pollution is a source of various water- borne diseases.

The text explains that with increasing use of varieties of fuels as the source of energy, there is a marked increase in emission of toxic gases into the atmosphere resulting in the pollution of air.

The text explains that combustion of fossil fuels, mining and industries are the main sources of air pollution.

The text explains that 98 India : People and Economy from the source of pollution, i.e. industrial areas, arteries of transportation, airport, etc.

The text explains that environmental pollution by solid wastes has now got significance because of enormous growth in the quantity of wastes generated from various sources.

The text explains that these discarded materials are also termed as refuse, garbage and rubbish, etc., and are disposed of from two sources : (i) household or domestic establishments, and (ii) industrial or commercial establishments.

The text explains that noise Pollution Noise pollution refers to the state of unbearable and uncomfortable to human beings which is caused by noise from different sources.

The text explains that the main sources of noise pollution are various factories, mechanised construction and demolition works, automobiles and aircraft, etc.

The text explains that of all these sources, the biggest nuisance is the noise produced by traffic, because its intensity and nature depend upon factors, such as the type of aircraft, vehicle, train and the condition of road, as well as, that of vehicle (in case of automobiles).

The text explains that the powerful logics, authentic studies and the pressure of people have brought a new lease of life to the twelve thousand residents of this village.

The text explains that these wastes should be treated as resource and utilised for generating energy and compost.

The text explains that that will put pressure on governments to make urban areas better places to live with optimum infrastructure facilities for desirable quality of life.

The text explains that due to low opportunities in smaller and medium cities, the poor people generally bypass these small cities and directly come to the mega cities for their livelihood.

The text explains that but Dharavi is a keeper of more sombre secrets than the revulsion it inspires in the rich; a revulsion, moreover, that is, in direct proportion to the role it serves in the creation of the wealth of Bombay.

The text explains that here most of the villages exist as appendix to the core urban centre forming its hinterland.

The text explains that land Degradation Land Degradation Land Degradation Land Degradation Land Degradation The pressure on agricultural land increases not only due to the limited availability but also by deterioration of quality of agricultural land.

The text explains that there are a few types of wastelands such as gullied /ravinous land, desertic or coastal sands, barren rocky areas, steep sloping land, and glacial areas, which are primarily caused by natural agents.

The text explains that 9.4 : Trees planted on Common Property Resources in Jhabua Source: Evaluation Report, Rajiv Gandhi Mission for Watershed Management, Government of Madhya Pradesh, 2002 Fig.

The text explains that 9.5 : Community Participation for Land Leveling in Common Property Resources in Jhabua (ASA, 2004) are caused by human action.

The text explains that the people suffer due to poverty which has been accentuated by the high rate of resource degradation, both forest and land.

The text explains that watershed Management Programmes acknowledge the linkage between land, water and vegetation and attempts to improve livelihoods of people through natural resource management and community participation.

The text explains that the Bhils in Petlawad block, for example, (Sat Rundi hamlet of Karravat village) through their own efforts, have revitalised large parts of common property resources.

The text explains that (See the section on CPR in chapter ‘Land Resources and Agriculture’).

The text explains that (ii) Describe the major source of air pollution.

The text explains that (i) Which one of the following river is highly polluted?

The text explains that (a) Brahmaputra (c) Yamuna (b) Satluj (d) Godavari (ii) Which one of the following deseases is caused by water pollution?

The text explains that (a) Conjunctivitis (c) Respiratory infections (b) Diarrhorea (d) Bronchitis (iii) Which one of the following is the cause of acid rain?

The text explains that (a) Water pollution (c) Noise pollution (b) Land pollution (d) Air pollution (iv) Push and pull factors are responsible for– (a) Migration (c) Slums (b) Land degradation (d) Air pollution 2 .

The text explains that (i) What is the difference between pollution and pollutants?

The text explains that (iii) Mention major problems associated with urban waste disposal in India.

The text explains that (iv) What are the effects of air pollution on human health.

The text explains that (i) Describe the nature of water pollution in India.

The text explains that (iii) Suggest measures for reduction of land degradation.

Source: NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy, Chapter 9: Planning and Sustainable Development

schemes.name: Namami Gange Programme Ganga
schemes.point: Namami Gange Programme Ganga, as a river, has national importance but the river requires cleaning by effectively controlling the pollution for its water. (NCERT Class 12 India People and Economy Ch9 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat GEOGRAPHICAL and PERSPECTIVE ON as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Planning and Sustainable Development; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Planning and Sustainable Development through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use GEOGRAPHICAL and PERSPECTIVE ON to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
